1: gam-'elleh mishle shelomoh — “these also are proverbs of Solomon.” The editorial superscription signals a distinct collection. He'etiqo (H6275, to transfer, to copy, to preserve) is the verb for the scribal activity of the “men of Hezekiah” ('anshei yechizqiyahu). The historicity of the Hezekiah editorial connection (c. 715–686 BCE) is accepted by most commentators; the scribal school attached to the palace preserved older Solomonic material. This verse is one of the most precise editorial attributions in the wisdom literature.
2: kevod 'elohim haster dabar — “the glory of God to conceal a matter.” Kavod (H3519, glory/weight) is used for the honor expressed through characteristic action; YHWH’s glory is expressed in concealment (haster, the infinitive of satar, H5641, to hide/conceal). The royal counterpart is investigation: ukhevod melakhim chaqor dabar — “and the glory of kings to search out a matter.” Chaqar (H2713, to search out, investigate, explore) is the verb for thorough examination. The aphorism encodes a theology of the divine-human epistemic asymmetry: YHWH’s mastery is displayed in what he keeps hidden; human mastery is displayed in what they can discover.
3: shamayim laroom ve-'aretz le'amoq — “the heavens for height, and the earth for depth.” The inaccessible extremes of vertical space (sky/depth) frame the inaccessible interior of the king’s heart: ve-lev melakhim ein cheqer — “and the heart of kings is unsearchable.” Ein cheqer (no searching/investigation possible) makes the royal heart as epistemically inaccessible as the cosmic extremes. The political wisdom of the aphorism: kings are not transparent, and their decisions come from depths that subjects cannot plumb.
4: hago sigim mikkasef — “remove dross from silver.” Sigim (H5509, dross, the slag separated from metal in smelting) is the impurity that, once removed, allows the silversmith to produce a vessel (keli). The metallurgical metaphor for moral purification is applied in v5 to the political realm: remove the wicked from before the king, and the throne is established on righteousness. The technical vocabulary of metalworking applied to governance is characteristically Solomonic.
5: hageh rasha' lifnei-melekh — “remove the wicked from before the king.” Hagah (H1898, here = to remove — related to the verb used of separating dross from silver in v4) makes explicit the parallel: wicked counselors are the dross of the royal court. Ve-yikkon be-tzedeq kis'o — “and his throne will be established on righteousness.” Kun in Niphal (to be established, to be secure, H3559) with tzedeq (righteousness, H6664) frames the stability of political rule as a moral outcome.
6: 'al-tit'addar lifnei-melekh — “do not exalt yourself before the king.” Pa'ar (H6286, to exalt oneself, to beautify/glorify oneself) in the Hithpael = self-glorification in the royal presence. Uvimqom gedolim al-ta'amod — “and do not stand in the place of great men.” The court wisdom of vv6-7 governs the behavior of those who seek royal favor: the voluntary assumption of a lower position is safer and ultimately more advantageous than the presumption of a higher one.
7: ki tov 'emor-lecha 'aleh hennah — “for it is better to be told, ‘come up here,’ than to be put lower.” The social mechanics of the royal court: amur-lecha (passive, “it is said to you”) is the invitation from above, more valuable than self-positioning. Lifnei nodiv 'asher ra'u 'einecha — “in the presence of a noble whom your eyes have seen.” The eyewitness qualifier grounds the aphorism in actual social experience rather than abstract principle.
8: 'al-tetze' la-riv mahir — “do not go out hastily to court.” Mahir (H4116, hastily, quickly) qualifies the danger: the problem is not litigation as such but premature litigation. Pen mah-ta'aseh be'acharitah — “lest you know not what to do in the end when your neighbor has put you to shame.” The shame scenario of v8 motivates the counsel of vv9-10: settle disputes privately rather than through public court, where humiliation is more likely.
9: rivekhah riv 'et-re'akha — “argue your case with your neighbor yourself.” The instruction to keep disputes between the parties rather than escalating to public court. Vesod 'acher 'al-tegal — “and do not reveal the secret of another.” Sod (H5475, secret, confidential counsel) is the information shared in private counsel; to broadcast it in a legal dispute is to compound the wrong. The wisdom tradition consistently values the repair of relationship over the vindication of rights.
10: pen yechasedkha shomea' — “lest he who hears it bring shame on you.” The social consequence of revealing secrets in litigation: dibbatekha lo' tashuv — “your evil reputation will not turn back.” Dibbah (H1681, defamation, evil report) once circulated cannot be retracted; the irreversibility of reputational damage is the warning. The wisdom tradition has a sober realism about the permanence of social facts.
11: tapuchim zahav be-maskiyot kasef — “golden apples in a silver setting.” Tapuach (H8598, apple — or possibly quince or apricot; the golden-colored fruit) in a maskiyot (H4906, carved/latticed setting, a filigree or embossed work) of silver is the image of the precisely crafted decorative object. The beauty of the combination — gold fruit in silver setting — is the standard of excellence against which the dabar dabar 'al-'ofanav (word spoken on its occasions/wheels — i.e., at the right turning-point) is measured. The phrase “spoken on its wheels” suggests a word that arrives at the perfectly timed moment.
12: nezem zahav ve-chaliy-paz — “a gold ring and a fine-gold ornament.” Nezem (H5141, ring — typically a nose-ring or ear-ring) of gold and a chali paz (H2481, ornament of paz, the finest refined gold) are luxury jewelry items. These represent a wise rebuker (mochiach chakham) to a listening ear ('ozen shoma'at): the skillful rebuke that is received is as valuable as the finest jewelry and, like a ring, stays with the person who receives it.
13: ke-tzinnat-sheleg be-yom qatzir — “like the coolness of snow in harvest time.” Tzinnah (H6793, cold, the cold feeling) of sheleg (snow, H7950) during the harvest (qatzir, the hot dry season when snow itself is absent but cold water from snow-melt was stored) is the most refreshing experience imaginable in that climate. Applied to the tzir ne'eman (faithful messenger, H6735) — he refreshes (meshiv) the soul (nefesh) of his senders. The nefesh (H5315, the whole person/throat/life-force) refreshed by a reliable messenger captures the anxiety that surrounds delegated tasks.
14: 'anashim ruchot ve-geshem 'ain — “clouds and wind but no rain.” The meteorological frustration of the drought cloud (promising rain that never comes) is applied to ish mit'hallel bemattan shaqer — “a man who boasts of a gift that does not exist.” Mit'hallel (H1984 Hithpael, to boast, to brag) of a non-existent gift (mattan shaqer, false gift) matches anticipation to disappointment. The social failure of the promise-breaker has the same structure as the agricultural frustration of the dry cloud.
15: be-'orekh ru'ach yefutteh qatzin — “by long patience a ruler is persuaded.” 'Orekh ru'ach (long spirit, patient endurance) as a persuasive strategy for dealing with those in power. Lashon rakkah tishbor-garem — “and a soft tongue breaks a bone.” The paradox: the physically yielding tongue (rakkah, H7390, soft, gentle) is more effective at breaking the hard (garem, bone, H1634 — the hardest material of the body) than force. The inversion of expected physical causation is the wisdom insight about the power of patient, gentle speech.
16: devash matzata 'echol daieka — “if you find honey, eat only what you need.” Dai (H1767, sufficiency, enough) qualifies the honey-consumption: wisdom in eating is knowing when to stop. Pen-tis'benu vahaqe'ota — “lest you be full and vomit it up.” The physical outcome of over-indulgence (qe', vomiting) provides the vivid warning against the pleasures that become repulsive when taken to excess. The honey metaphor applies broadly to any good thing that becomes harmful through excess.
17: hoker ragelcha mibeit re'echa — “let your foot be seldom in your neighbor’s house.” Yaqar (H3365, rare, precious — the Hiphil here = to make rare/scarce) applied to one’s presence at a neighbor’s house is the idiom for maintaining appropriate social distance. Pen-yisba'akha ushne'akha — “lest he be full of you and hate you.” The social observation: familiarity that exceeds the natural limit produces saturation (sava', H7646) and then aversion (sana', hatred). Wisdom about social relationships includes knowing when one’s presence is welcome and when it has become an imposition.
18: mefitz ve-cherev ve-chetz shannun — “a club, a sword, and a sharp arrow.” The three weapons of war are the simile for 'ish 'oneh bere'ehu 'ed shaqer — “a man who bears false witness against his neighbor.” The false witness in the legal system functions as a weapon of assault: the language is not metaphorical exaggeration but a precise moral equivalence. Judicial perjury is assault and attempted murder, not merely dishonesty.
19: shen ro'ah ve-regel mu'adeth — “a broken tooth and a faltering foot.” Two sources of pain and mechanical failure (the tooth that cannot chew, the foot that cannot bear weight) are the image of mivtach boged be-yom tzarah — “confidence in an unfaithful man in a time of trouble.” Boged (H898, treacherous/faithless person) at precisely the moment of crisis (yom tzarah): the failure comes exactly when the support was needed most. The physical similes capture both the pain of the failure and its practical uselessness.
20: mafshit beged be-yom qarah — “one who removes a garment on a cold day.” The social obtuseness of the wrong response to a context: stripping clothing in the cold is not merely useless but actively harmful. Applied in parallel to shir shir 'al-lev ra' — “singing songs to a heavy heart.” Lev ra' (heavy/evil heart, a heart in distress) confronted with cheerful singing suffers a double insult: the tone of the response is the opposite of what the situation requires. The wisdom of social responsiveness includes reading the emotional temperature of others.
21: im ra'ev sone'akha ha'achilenu lechem — “if your enemy is hungry, give him bread to eat.” Sone'akha (the one who hates you, your enemy, H8130) as the specific recipient of charity extends the social ethic beyond those one likes. The specificity of the command — ha'achilenu lechem (give him bread to eat) and hashqehu mayim (give him water to drink) — grounds the abstract enemy-love in concrete material provision. The NT quotation in Rom 12:20 keeps these specific material forms.
22: ki gechalim 'atah chotheh 'al-ro'sho — “for you will heap burning coals on his head.” Gechalim (H1513, glowing coals, burning embers) heaped on the head is the most contested phrase in the chapter. Interpretations include: (a) the coals of shame — the enemy’s shame at receiving unexpected kindness; (b) the coals of refining fire — the act of kindness that burns away enmity and produces change; (c) an Egyptian ritual of public penitence where a basin of coals was carried on the head as a sign of contrition. The MKT preserves the ambiguity. Ve-YHWH yeshallem-lakh — “and the LORD will reward you”: the theological grounding of the ethic.
23: ru'ach tzafon techollel geshem — “the north wind brings rain.” In Palestine, the north wind typically brings dry weather; some commentators suggest the text may reflect a different climatic observation or a reference to northwest winds. Ufanim nilozah lashon sater — “and a slanderous tongue brings angry faces.” The structure is cause-and-effect: the lashon sater (tongue of secrecy, the one who gossips in private) inevitably produces panim nilozah (angry countenance). The meteorological certainty of the wind-rain connection is applied to the social certainty of the slander-anger connection.
24: tov shevet 'al-pinnot gag — “better to live in a corner of the roof.” The corner of the flat-roofed Palestinian house (exposed to the elements, cramped) is preferred to mimishqal beith chaver — “a house shared with a quarrelsome wife.” Midyan (H4066, contention/strife — used also in Prov 21:9) is the word for the contentious personality. The repetition of this aphorism (cf. 21:9) reflects its status as an established unit in the oral wisdom tradition. The hyperbole (rooftop > full house) encodes the wisdom that relational peace exceeds material comfort.
25: mayim qarim 'al-nefesh 'ayefah — “cold water to a weary soul.” Nefesh 'ayefah (H5315 + H5889, a weary/exhausted nephesh) uses the whole-person vocabulary of Hebrew anthropology: the nefesh is not merely the inner spirit but the entire embodied person whose throat is parched with thirst. Shmu'ah tovah me'eretz marchaq — “good news from a distant land”: the comparison equates the physical relief of cold water with the emotional and social relief of positive tidings from far away. The word shmu'ah (H8052, report, news, rumor) is the term used in Isa 53:1 (“who has believed our report?”).
26: ma'ayan nidrakh umaqor mashhath — “a muddied spring and a polluted fountain.” Nidrakh (H7512 in MT, some manuscripts have nirpas = trampled) describes a water source rendered unusable by disturbance. The image applies to tzaddiq mat lifnei rasha' — “a righteous man who gives way before the wicked.” The corruption of a water source destroys its usefulness; the collapse of the righteous before wickedness destroys the community’s moral resource. The righteous person’s role in the community is compared to a spring: essential, potentially ruinable.
27: 'akhol devash harbeh lo'-tov — “it is not good to eat too much honey.” The honey-excess warning of v16 returns as a frame for the final aphorism: ve-cheqer khevod-khavod — “and to seek one’s own glory is not glory.” The second clause is syntactically terse; the most natural reading parallels the honey: just as too much honey is bad, so too much self-promotion collapses the glory it seeks. Kavod pursued directly dissolves; kavod that comes from others or from achievement is the only genuine article.
28: 'ein ma'atsar le-rucho — “a man who has no control over his spirit.” Ma'atsar (H4623, restraint, control) is the capacity to contain and direct the ru'ach (H7307, spirit, breath, disposition). The city without walls ('ir paruzah 'ein chomah) is the political image for the person without self-containment: militarily indefensible, permanently vulnerable, exposed to every attack. The architectural-political metaphor is applied to the inner life of the person who has not cultivated the capacity for self-restraint.
